title: Blood Glucose Regulation
description: The pancreas as a thermostat — watch insulin and glucagon maintain blood sugar in a tight range through negative feedback.

meal_size.label: Meal Size
meal_size.description: How much glucose enters the bloodstream

insulin_response.label: Insulin Response
insulin_response.description: Pancreatic beta cell sensitivity (low = Type 2 diabetes pattern)

narration.text: After a meal, glucose (yellow hexagons) floods the bloodstream. Blood glucose spikes. Pancreatic beta cells detect this and release insulin (green circles).
Insulin acts as a key — it binds to receptors on cells, unlocking glucose channels. Cells absorb glucose for energy. Blood levels fall.
As glucose falls, the insulin signal weakens. The pancreas reduces insulin release. This is negative feedback — the system corrects itself.
If blood glucose drops too low, the pancreas releases glucagon instead — the opposite hormone. It signals the liver to release stored glucose. Balance restored.
In Type 1 diabetes, beta cells are destroyed — no insulin is produced. In Type 2, cells become resistant to insulin — the keys no longer fit the locks.
This tight 4-8 mmol/L blood glucose range is critical. Too high: damages blood vessels. Too low: brain stops functioning within minutes.
